---
title: "Who's that Pokémon?"
subtitle: "A research on the most competitive Pokémon // A.Y. 2025-2026"
author: 
  - Maria Briones
  - Riccardo Dondi
  - Sayantan Mandal
format: 
  revealjs :
    embed-resources: true
    width: 1200
    height: 1000
    margin: 0.04
    min-scale: 0.2
    max-scale: 2.0
theme: solarized
transition: slide
incremental: true
scrollable: true
---

## Our mission: catch them all!

Our main goal is to provide some insights on the Pokémon world to help parents to advice their children on how to catch them all.

![POKEDEX of Sinnoh region](/images/727px-Pokédex_Sinnoh_Pt.webp){width="45%" fig-align="center"}

## The data

![](/images/poke_f.gif){fig-align="center" width="75"}

-   We will be using the [Pokémon](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-04-01/readme.md) dataset, which contains information about 949 pokemons on 22 variables.

```{r}
#| label: set-up

#load pacman library to load all the libraries and install if needed
library(pacman)

# Load libraries
p_load(tidyverse, #for data manipulation
        ggplot2, #for plotting
        tidytuesdayR, #to load the data
        forcats, #for factor manipulation
        farver, #for color manipulation
        knitr, #for appealing tables
        kableExtra, #for appealing tables
        skimr, #for data exploration
        viridisLite, #for color palette
        ggthemes
        )
```

```{r}
#| label: data-import

# import data pokemon from tidytuesdayR
pokemon_df <- tt_load(2025, week = 13)$pokemon_df
```

- DATA CLEANING: We removed NA values from generation as they belonged to special edition pokemons and discarded irrelevant variables like color, height and egg group, among others.

## Our Variables

```{r}
#| label: data-cleaning

# drop columns that we don't need and which have many NAs
pokemon_df <- pokemon_df |>
  select(-c(height, weight, base_experience, color_2, color_f, egg_group_2, species_id, url_icon, url_image))

# drop pokemons with missing generation_id
pokemon_df = pokemon_df |> 
  filter(!is.na(generation_id))

#manage NAs in type 2
pokemon_df = pokemon_df |> 
  mutate(pokemon = str_to_title(pokemon), #gives some format to Pokémon names.
         type_2 = ifelse(is.na(type_2), "none", type_2) |> str_to_title(),
         type_1 = str_to_title(type_1))
```

-   **id** and **pokemon**: the id (num) and name (chr) of the Pokémon.
-   **type_1** and **type_2**: the primary and secondary type of the Pokémon (chr).
-   **hp**: the health points of the Pokémon (num).
-   **attack**: the attack points of the Pokémon (num).
-   **defense**: the defense points of the Pokémon (num).
-   **special_attack**: the special attack points of the Pokémon (num).
-   **special_defense**: the special defense points of the Pokémon (num).
-   **speed**: the speed points of the Pokémon (num).
-   **generation_id**: the generation on wich the Pokémon was introduced (num).

## The insights

We worked mainly on 3 insights:

1.  **Pokémon distribution by generation** (table)
2.  **Number of Pokemons by type and generation** (table)
3.  **Stats of Dual-type vs Single-type Pokemons** (table)

![](/images/pokemon-confused.gif){fig-align="center" width="60%"}

## 1. Distribution by generation

-   We want to understand:

    -   Which generation has the most pokemons?
    -   Which generation has the most powerful pokemons?

-   A table should be sufficient to answer these questions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1. Distribution by generation

```{r}
#| label: pokemon-distribution

# to a better analysis we create to combined variables
pokemon_df = pokemon_df |> 
  mutate(comb_attack = attack + special_attack,
         comb_defense = defense + special_defense)
         
#summarise pokemon distribution by generation and main stats
interest_stats = pokemon_df |> 
  group_by(generation_id) |> 
  summarise(n = n(),
            avg_hp = mean(hp, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_attack = mean(comb_attack, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_defense = mean(comb_defense, na.rm = TRUE),
            avg_speed = mean(speed, na.rm = TRUE)
)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-poke_distribution
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Pokemon average stats by generation"

# Identify generations with the highest number of pokemons
top_gen <- rank(-interest_stats$n) <= 3

#identify the top generation on each stat
top_hp <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_hp) <= 3
top_attack <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_attack) <= 3
top_defense <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_defense) <= 3
top_speed <- rank(-interest_stats$avg_speed) <= 3


turbo=turbo(n=3, begin= 0.8, end=0.99, direction = 1) #3 color from turbo palette

# Create background color vector (only for top 3)
gen_colors <- ifelse(top_gen, spec_color(interest_stats$n, end = 0.5), "transparent") #default viridis
hp_colors <- ifelse(top_hp, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_hp, palette=turbo), "black")
at_colors <- ifelse(top_attack, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_attack, palette=turbo),"black")
def_colors <- ifelse(top_defense, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_defense, palette=turbo),"black")
sp_colors <- ifelse(top_speed, spec_color(interest_stats$avg_speed, palette=turbo),"black")


interest_stats |>
  kable(digits = 2,
        col.names = c("Generation", "Count", "HP", "C. Attack", "C. Defense", "Speed")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed", "responsive"),
                full_width = FALSE, position = "center") |> #some styling options for the table
  column_spec(2, color ="black", background = gen_colors) |> #backwround color for 2 column (number of pokemons)
  column_spec(3, color = hp_colors, bold = top_hp) |> #color and bold for hp column
  column_spec(4, color = at_colors, bold = top_attack) |> 
  column_spec(5, color = def_colors, bold = top_defense) |> 
  column_spec(6, color = sp_colors, bold = top_speed)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1. Outcome

-   The most populated generations are **the 1st, 3rd and 5th**, highlighted in blue in the table.

-   Nevertheless, the most powerfull generation considering all four stats (combined attack, combined defense, hp and speed) is **the 4th generation**.

![The starters of 4th gen](/images/Starters_vectorial-removebg-preview.png){fig-align="center"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2. Number of Pokemons by type and generation

Are some types or combination of types more common than others? 

```{r}
#| label: tbl-type_distribution
#| tbl-cap: "Number of Pokemons by type and generation"

#table for count of pokemon by type and generation 
pokemon_df |> 
  group_by(generation_id, type_1) |> 
  summarise(n = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = generation_id, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(Total = rowSums(across(where(is.numeric)))) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Primary type", "Gen 1", "Gen 2", "Gen 3", "Gen 4", "Gen 5", "Gen 6",
                      "Gen 7", "Total")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed", "responsive"),
                full_width = F, position = "center")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2. Outcome

-   The most common types are **Water**, **Normal** and **Grass**. However, these types are not equally distributed across generations. 

-   For example, the water type is more common in the 1st generation than in the 4th generation and Normal type is the rarest main type on the 6th generation.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3. Dual vs Single type Pokemons

Does dual type Pokémon performes better than single type?

-   To answer this question, we should look at the average power stats of both single and dual type pokemons.

$power = hp + attack + defense + sp.attack + sp.defense + speed$

![](/images/pokemon-charjabug.gif){width="50%" fig-align="center"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3a. Category comparison

```{r}
#| label: dual_analysis 
#| tbl-cap: "Summary Power Statistics by Dual-Type Status"

#data for dual analysis
df <- pokemon_df |>
  mutate(
    type_2       = na_if(type_2, "None"),
    is_dual_type = !is.na(type_2),
    power_stats  = hp + attack + defense + special_attack + special_defense + speed
  )

dual_summary <- df |>
  group_by(is_dual_type) |>
  summarise(
    n            = n(),
    mean_power   = round(mean(power_stats), 1),
    median_power = round(median(power_stats), 1),
    sd_power     = round(sd(power_stats), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

dual_summary |>
  kable(col.names = c("Is Dual?", "Count", "Mean Power", "Median Power", "Power SD")
  ) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)

```

Dual-type Pokémon average **`r dual_summary |> filter(is_dual_type) |> pull(mean_power)`** power stats vs **`r dual_summary |> filter(!is_dual_type) |> pull(mean_power)`** for single-types — a \~35-point (8.56%) gap.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3b. Most powerfull primary types

```{r}
#| label: tbl-primary_dualtype_stats
#| tbl-cap: "Mean Power Stats by Primary Type and Dual-Type Status"

type_dual <- df |>
  group_by(type_1, is_dual_type) |>
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    mean_power = round(mean(power_stats), 1),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n >= 5) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_power))


type_dual |> slice_head(n = 10) |>
  kable(col.names = c("Primary Type", "Is Dual?", "Count", "Mean Power")
  ) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE)
```


------------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3b. Most powerfull primary types

Dragon dual-types lead all groups at 568 power average. Fire types follows far behind with and average of 490.5 on power stats.

![](/images/Dragon%20pokemons.webp){fig-align="center" width="50%"}

![](/images/strong_c.gif){fig-align="center" width="40%"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Visualizations :

![](/images/poke-graph.png){fig-align="center" width="254"}

The previous insights are complemented by graph analysis.

-   **Type proportion on the 7 generations** (heatmap)
-   **Distribution of stats by type** (bar + line plot)
-   **Single vs Dual-type stat distributions** (violin - plot)

## Type proportion

-   Considering the 18 main types and the 19 secondary types (including “None”), there are **342 possible combinations** of types.

-   Which combinations of types are more common and which are more rare?

-   To better understand the proportion of types we can use a heatmap: the more intense the color, the more pokemons of that type combination there are.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Type proportion

```{r}
#| label: type-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Normalized proportion of pokemons by types"

# count of pokemon by type 1 and type 2
poke_type <- pokemon_df|> 
  group_by(type_1, type_2) |> 
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  complete(type_1, type_2, fill = list(n = 0)) |>
  ungroup() |> 
  mutate(prop = n * 100 / sum(n), 
         norm_prop = case_when (prop==0 ~ -2.1,
                                TRUE ~ log(prop))
        )

poke_type <- poke_type |>
  mutate(type_2 = fct_relevel(type_2, "None")) #we make it a factor so "None" appears first

#graph 
ggplot(poke_type, aes(x = type_2, y = type_1, fill = norm_prop)) +
  geom_tile(color = "lightgrey") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "#B22222") +
  labs(
    title = "Normalized proportion of pokemons by types",
    x = "Secondary Type",
    y = "Main Type",
    fill = "Norm. Prop."
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Outcome

Assuming that each pokémon species distributed homogeneously across the world (strong assumption), we could say that the most abuntant pokemons belong to the **Water-None** combination. 

For Double-type, the most common combinations are: **Normal-Flying** (like Pidgey), **Grass-Poison** (like Bulbasaur), **Bug-Flying** (like Butterfree).

![Squirtle gang](/images/squirtle-pokemon.gif){fig-align="center" width="230"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Distribution of stats by type

To have a even more clear view on the Pokémon world, we analyzed also the distribution of stats grouped by type to see which are the most or less effective ones.

-   We used a bar-plot for the combined attack and defense stats overlaped by a line plot for the health-points and speed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Distribution of stats by type

```{r}
#| label: type-stats
#| fig-width : 12
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-cap: "Average Stats by Primary Type"

#new interest stats by type
# 1. Aggregate data by Type only
type_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(type_1) %>%
  summarise(
    # Calculate the means of the sums directly
    attack_total  = mean(attack + special_attack, na.rm = TRUE),
    defense_total = mean(defense + special_defense, na.rm = TRUE),
    speed         = mean(speed, na.rm = TRUE),
    hp            = mean(hp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(attack_total)) %>%
  mutate(type_1 = factor(type_1, levels = type_1))

# 2. Pivot (Note: Ensure the names match your new columns)
type_long <- type_summary %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(attack_total, defense_total),
    names_to = "stat_name",
    values_to = "stat_value"
  )

# 3. Plot
ggplot() +
  geom_col(
    data = type_long,
    aes(x = type_1, y = stat_value, fill = stat_name),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), alpha = 0.8
  ) +
  # Dashed line for Speed
  geom_line(
    data = type_summary,
    aes(x = type_1, y = speed, group = 1, color = "Avg Speed"),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  # Solid line for HP
  geom_line(
    data = type_summary,
    aes(x = type_1, y = hp, group = 1, color = "Avg HP"),
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +
  geom_point(data = type_summary, aes(x = type_1, y = speed, color = "Avg Speed"), size = 3, shape = 16) +
  geom_point(data = type_summary, aes(x = type_1, y = hp, color = "Avg HP"), size = 3, shape = 15) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("attack_total" = "#EF5350", "defense_total" = "#42A5F5"), # Updated names
    labels = c("Total Attack", "Total Defense"), 
    name = "Base Stats"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Avg Speed" = "#FFA800", "Avg HP" = "#059142"),
    name = "Trends"
  ) +
  theme_clean() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))+
  labs(
    title = "Average Stats by Primary Type",
    x = "Primary Type",
    y = "Average Value"
  )

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Single vs Dual type stats distribution

Coming back to the Dual-type analysis, we perform a violin chart to visualize the distribution of our summarising power stat across the different primary type categories.

-   The plot shows that dual type pokemons have a higher median and a wider distribution of power stats compared to single type pokemons.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Single vs Dual type stats distribution

```{r}
#| label: violin-plot
#| echo: false
#| fig-width : 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-cap: "Power Base Stats by Primary Type — Single vs Dual-typing"

# Order types by median power stats (dual-type)
type_order <- df |>
  filter(is_dual_type) |>
  group_by(type_1) |>
  summarise(med = median(power_stats), .groups = "drop") |>
  arrange(desc(med)) |>
  pull(type_1)

df |>
  mutate(
    type_1       = factor(type_1, levels = type_order),
    typing_label = if_else(is_dual_type, "Dual-type", "Single-type")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = type_1, y = power_stats, fill = typing_label)) +
  geom_violin(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.85),
    alpha = 0.65, colour = NA, scale = "width"
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    aes(group = typing_label),
    fun = median, geom = "point", shape = 21,
    colour = "white", fill = "white", size = 1.5,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.85)
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Single-type" = "#4FC3F7", "Dual-type" = "#FF6B35")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(100, 800, 100)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Power Base Stats by Primary Type — Single vs Dual-typing",
    subtitle = "Types ordered by dual-type median. White dots = median.",
    x = NULL, y = "Power Base Stats", fill = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.background  = element_rect(fill = "#0D0D1A", colour = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#0D0D1A", colour = NA),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "#1E1E3A"),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    axis.text   = element_text(colour = "#9E9EA0"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1, face = "bold"),
    axis.title  = element_text(colour = "#C0C0D0"),
    plot.title    = element_text(colour = "white", face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "#7B7B9E", size = 10),
    legend.background = element_rect(fill = "#13132A", colour = NA),
    legend.text  = element_text(colour = "white"),
    legend.position = "top"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conclusion

Some battle-tested advice:

-   Dual-type Pokémon are worth the extra effort (Dragon-dual-types are the best ones);
-   The 4th generation is a goldmine of powerful Pokemons, so consider focusing on the Sinnoh region;
-   Always keep an eye on the types synergy and on their rareness.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Who's our Pokémon?

The most competitive Pokémon is **Garchomp**: a Dragon-Ground dual-type from the 4th generation with a stat power of 608 points, 130 attack and 95 defense, but...

![](/images/garchomp.png){fig-align="center"}

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Who's our Pokémon?

At the end, our choice was, is and will always be **Pikachu ⚡**!

![The best pokemon ever](/images/Pikachu.png){fig-align="center" width="45%"}
